reasoning:
The historical ACLED fatality baseline for Honduras typically sits around 20-50 per month (for example, there were ~500 civilian fatalities linked to organized violence over 11 months in 2023). Based on the resolution criteria of more than 10 times the baseline (1 + 30-day average), a positive resolution would require anywhere from 200 to over 500 fatalities in a single 30-day window, depending on recent averages. Such numbers are astronomically high and essentially imply massive civil war dynamics entirely incongruent with current reports. 

While the prompt cites a "most recent known value of 1.000" (suggesting exactly 0 fatalities recorded in the past 360 days up to Dec 25, 2025), this indicates either an ACLED data pause, a lag in processing, or an isolated reporting artifact. Because ACLED regularly updates and back-codes its data, the final benchmark values drawn near the resolution dates will likely reflect the true historical average. If back-coded, the baseline restores to historical norms (making a 10x spike impossible). If not back-coded, the 30-day window fatalities will likely also be 0, which is undeniably not > 10. Thus, in all scenarios, the chance of this condition being strictly met is exceptionally low.
